resources](#related-resources) Skills extend what Claude can do. Create a `SKILL.md` file with instructions, and Claude adds it to its toolkit. Claude uses skills when relevant, or you can invoke one directly with `/skill-name`. For built-in commands like `/help` and `/compact`, see [interactive mode](/docs/en/interactive-mode#built-in-commands).**Custom slash commands have been merged into skills.** A file at `.claude/commands/review.md` and a skill at `.claude/skills/review/SKILL.md` both create `/review` and work the same way. Your existing `.claude/commands/` files keep working. Skills add optional features: a directory for supporting files, frontmatter to [control whether you or Claude invokes them](#control-who-invokes-a-skill), and the ability for Claude to load them automatically when relevant. Claude Code skills follow the [Agent Skills](https://agentskills.io) open standard, which works across multiple AI tools. Claude Code extends the standard with additional features like [invocation control](#control-who-invokes-a-skill), [subagent execution](#run-skills-in-a-subagent), and [dynamic context injection](#inject-dynamic-context). ## [​](#getting-started) Getting started ### [​](#create-your-first-skill) Create your first skill This example creates a skill that teaches Claude to explain code using visual diagrams and analogies. Since it uses default frontmatter, Claude can load it automatically when you ask how something works, or you can invoke it directly with `/explain-code`. 1 Create the skill directory Create a directory for the skill in your personal skills folder. Personal skills are available across all your projects. Report incorrect code Copy Ask AI ``` mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/explain-code ``` 2 Write SKILL.md Every skill needs a `SKILL.md` file with two parts: YAML frontmatter (between `---` markers) that tells Claude when to use the skill, and markdown content with instructions Claude follows when the skill is invoked. The `name` field becomes the `/slash-command`, and the `description` helps Claude decide when to load it automatically.Create `~/.claude/skills/explain-code/SKILL.md`: Report incorrect code Copy Ask AI ``` --- name: explain-code description: Explains code with visual diagrams and analogies. Use when explaining how code works, teaching about a codebase, or when the user asks "how does this work?" --- When explaining code, always include: 1. **Start with an analogy**: Compare the code

steps](#next-steps) Subagents are specialized AI assistants that handle specific types of tasks. Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, specific tool access, and independent permissions. When Claude encounters a task that matches a subagent’s description, it delegates to that subagent, which works independently and returns results. If you need multiple agents working in parallel and communicating with each other, see [agent teams](/docs/en/agent-teams) instead. Subagents work within a single session; agent teams coordinate across separate sessions. Subagents help you: * **Preserve context** by keeping exploration and implementation out of your main conversation * **Enforce constraints** by limiting which tools a subagent can use * **Reuse configurations** across projects with user-level subagents * **Specialize behavior** with focused system prompts for specific domains * **Control costs** by routing tasks to faster, cheaper models like Haiku Claude uses each subagent’s description to decide when to delegate tasks. When you create a subagent, write a clear description so Claude knows when to use it. Claude Code includes several built-in subagents like **Explore**, **Plan**, and **general-purpose**. You can also create custom subagents to handle specific tasks. This page covers the [built-in subagents](#built-in-subagents), [how to create your own](#quickstart-create-your-first-subagent), [full configuration options](#configure-subagents), [patterns for working with subagents](#work-with-subagents), and [example subagents](#example-subagents). ## [​](#built-in-subagents) Built-in subagents Claude Code includes built-in subagents that Claude automatically uses when appropriate. Each inherits the parent conversation’s permissions with additional tool restrictions. * Explore * Plan * General-purpose * Other A fast, read-only agent optimized for searching and analyzing codebases. * **Model**: Haiku (fast, low-latency) * **Tools**: Read-only tools (denied access to Write and Edit tools) * **Purpose**: File discovery, code search, codebase exploration Claude delegates to Explore when it needs to search or understand a codebase

Adding it to your `settings.json` enables autocomplete and inline validation in VS Code, Cursor, and any other editor that supports JSON schema validation. ### [​](#available-settings) Available settings `settings.json` supports a number of options: | Key | Description | Example | | --- | --- | --- | | `apiKeyHelper` | Custom script, to be executed in `/bin/sh`, to generate an auth value. This value will be sent as `X-Api-Key` and `Authorization: Bearer` headers for model requests | `/bin/generate_temp_api_key.sh` | | `cleanupPeriodDays` | Sessions inactive for longer than this period are deleted at startup. Setting to `0` immediately deletes all sessions. (default: 30 days) | `20` | | `companyAnnouncements` | Announcement to display to users at startup. If multiple announcements are provided, they will be cycled through at random. | `["Welcome to Acme Corp! Review our code guidelines at docs.acme.com"]` | | `env` | Environment variables that will be applied to every session | `{"FOO": "bar"}` | | `attribution` | Customize attribution for git commits and pull requests. See [Attribution settings](#attribution-settings) | `{"commit": "🤖 Generated with Claude Code", "pr": ""}` | | `includeCoAuthoredBy` | **Deprecated**: Use `attribution` instead. Whether to include the `co-authored-by Claude` byline in git commits and pull requests (default: `true`) | `false` | | `permissions` | See table below for structure of permissions. | | | `hooks` | Configure custom commands to run at lifecycle events. See [hooks documentation](/docs/en/hooks) for format | See [hooks](/docs/en/hooks) | | `disableAllHooks` | Disable all [hooks](/docs/en/hooks) and any custom [status line](/docs/en/statusline) | `true` | | `allowManagedHooksOnly` | (Managed settings only) Prevent loading of user, project, and plugin hooks. Only allows managed hooks and SDK hooks. See [Hook configuration](#hook-configuration) | `true` | | `allowManagedPermissionRulesOnly` | (Managed settings only) Prevent user and project settings from defining `allow`, `ask`, or `deny` permission rules. Only rules in managed settings apply. See [Managed-only settings](/docs/en/permissions#managed-only-settings) | `true` | | `model` | Override the default model to use for Claude Code | `"claude-sonnet-4-6"` | | `availableModels` | Restrict which models users can select via `/model`, `--model`, Config tool, or `ANTHROPIC_MODEL`. Does not affect the Default option. See [Restrict model selection](/docs/en/model-config#restrict-model-selection) | `["sonnet", "haiku"]` | | `otelHeadersHelper` | Script to generate dynamic OpenTelemetry headers. Runs at startup and periodically (see [Dynamic headers](/docs/en/monitoring-usage#dynamic-headers)) | `/bin/generate_otel_headers.sh` | | `statusLine` | Configure a custom status line to display context. See [`statusLine` documentation](/docs/en/statusline) | `{"type": "command", "command": "~/.claude/statusline.sh"}` | | `fileSuggestion` | Configure a custom script for `@` file autocomplete. records.answer:
To configure custom output styles in Claude Code using frontmatter syntax, follow these steps based on the provided context:

1. **Create a Markdown file** with frontmatter and custom instructions. The file structure should look like this:
   ```markdown
   ---
   name: My Custom Style
   description: A brief description of what this style does, to be displayed to the user
   keep-coding-instructions: false
   ---
   # Custom Style Instructions
   You are an interactive CLI tool that helps users with software engineering tasks. [Your custom instructions here...]
   ## Specific Behaviors
   [Define how the assistant should behave in this style...]
   ```

2. **Save the file** in one of the supported directories:
   - User-level: `~/.claude/output-styles/`
   - Project-level: `.claude/output-styles/`

3. **Frontmatter fields**:
   - `name`: The style's identifier (defaults to the file name if omitted).
   - `description`: A brief description for UI display (optional).
   - `keep-coding-instructions`: Boolean to retain coding-specific instructions (default `false`).

4. **Custom instructions** are added after the frontmatter, defining the style's behavior and formatting rules.

This method directly modifies Claude Code’s system prompt, overriding default behaviors while preserving core functionalities like script execution and TODO tracking.

an interactive CLI tool that helps users with software engineering tasks. [Your custom instructions here...] ## Specific Behaviors [Define how the assistant should behave in this style...] ``` You can save these files at the user level (`~/.claude/output-styles`) or project level (`.claude/output-styles`). ### [​](#frontmatter) Frontmatter Output style files support frontmatter, useful for specifying metadata about the command: | Frontmatter | Purpose | Default | | --- | --- | --- | | `name` | Name of the output style, if not the file name | Inherits from file name | | `description` | Description of the output style. Used only in the UI of `/output-style` | None | | `keep-coding-instructions` | Whether to keep the parts of Claude Code’s system prompt related to coding. | false | ## [​](#comparisons-to-related-features) Comparisons to related features ### [​](#output-styles-vs-claude-md-vs-—append-system-prompt) Output Styles vs. CLAUDE.md vs. —append-system-prompt Output styles completely “turn off” the parts of Claude Code’s default system prompt specific to software engineering. Neither CLAUDE.md nor `--append-system-prompt` edit Claude Code’s default system prompt. CLAUDE.md adds the contents as a user message *following* Claude Code’s default system prompt. `--append-system-prompt` appends the content to the system prompt. ### [​](#output-styles-vs-agents) Output Styles vs. [Agents](/docs/en/sub-agents) Output styles directly affect the main agent loop and only affect the system prompt. Agents are invoked to handle specific tasks and can include additional settings like the model to use, the tools they have available, and some context about when to use the agent. ### [​](#output-styles-vs-skills) Output Styles vs. [Skills](/docs/en/skills) Output styles modify how Claude responds (formatting, tone, structure) and are always active once selected. Skills are task-specific prompts that you invoke with `/skill-name` or that Claude loads automatically when relevant. Use output styles for consistent formatting preferences; use skills for reusable workflows and tasks. Was this page helpful? YesNo [Extend Claude with skills](/docs/en/skills)[Automate with hooks](/docs/en/hooks-guide) Assistant Responses are generated using AI and may contain mistakes.

There are two additional built-in output styles focused on teaching you the codebase and how Claude operates: * **Explanatory**: Provides educational “Insights” in between helping you complete software engineering tasks. Helps you understand implementation choices and codebase patterns. * **Learning**: Collaborative, learn-by-doing mode where Claude will not only share “Insights” while coding, but also ask you to contribute small, strategic pieces of code yourself. Claude Code will add `TODO(human)` markers in your code for you to implement. ## [​](#how-output-styles-work) How output styles work Output styles directly modify Claude Code’s system prompt. * All output styles exclude instructions for efficient output (such as responding concisely). * Custom output styles exclude instructions for coding (such as verifying code with tests), unless `keep-coding-instructions` is true. * All output styles have their own custom instructions added to the end of the system prompt. * All output styles trigger reminders for Claude to adhere to the output style instructions during the conversation. ## [​](#change-your-output-style) Change your output style You can either: * Run `/output-style` to access a menu and select your output style (this can also be accessed from the `/config` menu) * Run `/output-style [style]`, such as `/output-style explanatory`, to directly switch to a style These changes apply to the [local project level](/docs/en/settings) and are saved in `.claude/settings.local.json`. You can also directly edit the `outputStyle` field in a settings file at a different level. ## [​](#create-a-custom-output-style) Create a custom output style Custom output styles are Markdown files with frontmatter and the text that will be added to the system prompt: Report incorrect code Copy Ask AI ``` --- name: My Custom Style description: A brief description of what this style does, to be displayed to the user --- # Custom Style Instructions You are an interactive CLI tool that helps users with software engineering tasks. [Your custom instructions here...] ## Specific Behaviors [Define how the assistant should behave in this style...] ``` You can save these files at the user level (`~/.claude/output-styles`) or project level (`.claude/output-styles`). ### [​](#frontmatter) Frontmatter Output style files support frontmatter,

resources](#related-resources) Skills extend what Claude can do. Create a `SKILL.md` file with instructions, and Claude adds it to its toolkit. Claude uses skills when relevant, or you can invoke one directly with `/skill-name`. For built-in commands like `/help` and `/compact`, see [interactive mode](/docs/en/interactive-mode#built-in-commands).**Custom slash commands have been merged into skills.** A file at `.claude/commands/review.md` and a skill at `.claude/skills/review/SKILL.md` both create `/review` and work the same way. Your existing `.claude/commands/` files keep working. Skills add optional features: a directory for supporting files, frontmatter to [control whether you or Claude invokes them](#control-who-invokes-a-skill), and the ability for Claude to load them automatically when relevant. Claude Code skills follow the [Agent Skills](https://agentskills.io) open standard, which works across multiple AI tools. Claude Code extends the standard with additional features like [invocation control](#control-who-invokes-a-skill), [subagent execution](#run-skills-in-a-subagent), and [dynamic context injection](#inject-dynamic-context). ## [​](#getting-started) Getting started ### [​](#create-your-first-skill) Create your first skill This example creates a skill that teaches Claude to explain code using visual diagrams and analogies. Since it uses default frontmatter, Claude can load it automatically when you ask how something works, or you can invoke it directly with `/explain-code`. 1 Create the skill directory Create a directory for the skill in your personal skills folder. Personal skills are available across all your projects. Report incorrect code Copy Ask AI ``` mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills/explain-code ``` 2 Write SKILL.md Every skill needs a `SKILL.md` file with two parts: YAML frontmatter (between `---` markers) that tells Claude when to use the skill, and markdown content with instructions Claude follows when the skill is invoked. The `name` field becomes the `/slash-command`, and the `description` helps Claude decide when to load it automatically.Create `~/.claude/skills/explain-code/SKILL.md`: Report incorrect code Copy Ask AI ``` --- name: explain-code description: Explains code with visual diagrams and analogies. Use when explaining how code works, teaching about a codebase, or when the user asks "how does this work?" --- When explaining code, always include: 1. **Start with an analogy**: Compare the code

[Example subagents](#example-subagents) * [Code reviewer](#code-reviewer) * [Debugger](#debugger) * [Data scientist](#data-scientist) * [Database query validator](#database-query-validator) * [Next steps](#next-steps) Subagents are specialized AI assistants that handle specific types of tasks. Each subagent runs in its own context window with a custom system prompt, specific tool access, and independent permissions. When Claude encounters a task that matches a subagent’s description, it delegates to that subagent, which works independently and returns results. If you need multiple agents working in parallel and communicating with each other, see [agent teams](/docs/en/agent-teams) instead. Subagents work within a single session; agent teams coordinate across separate sessions. Subagents help you: * **Preserve context** by keeping exploration and implementation out of your main conversation * **Enforce constraints** by limiting which tools a subagent can use * **Reuse configurations** across projects with user-level subagents * **Specialize behavior** with focused system prompts for specific domains * **Control costs** by routing tasks to faster, cheaper models like Haiku Claude uses each subagent’s description to decide when to delegate tasks. When you create a subagent, write a clear description so Claude knows when to use it. Claude Code includes several built-in subagents like **Explore**, **Plan**, and **general-purpose**. You can also create custom subagents to handle specific tasks. This page covers the [built-in subagents](#built-in-subagents), [how to create your own](#quickstart-create-your-first-subagent), [full configuration options](#configure-subagents), [patterns for working with subagents](#work-with-subagents), and [example subagents](#example-subagents). ## [​](#built-in-subagents) Built-in subagents Claude Code includes built-in subagents that Claude automatically uses when appropriate. Each inherits the parent conversation’s permissions with additional tool restrictions. * Explore * Plan * General-purpose * Other A fast, read-only agent optimized for searching and analyzing codebases. * **Model**: Haiku (fast, low-latency) * **Tools**: Read-only tools (denied access to Write and Edit tools) * **Purpose**: File discovery, code search, codebase exploration Claude delegates to Explore when it needs to search or understand a codebase

Platform-specific shortcuts (e.g., macOS’s `Alt+B` for word navigation) require terminal configuration (e.g., enabling Option as Meta in iTerm2/Terminal.app). # Best Practices for Claude Code

Claude Code is an **agentic coding environment** that autonomously reads files, executes commands, and implements solutions based on natural language prompts—unlike traditional chatbots. It operates in a **three-phase workflow**: *exploration* (researching requirements), *planning* (designing solutions), and *execution* (writing/editing code). Key constraints revolve around **context window limits**, where excessive file/command outputs degrade performance. Best practices emphasize **structured workflows** (e.g., *explore → plan → code*), **granular context management** (rewinding, checkpoints, and aggressive pruning), and **rich prompt design** (specificity, structured `CLAUDE.md` files, and multi-agent collaboration). Advanced techniques include **subagents for investigation**, **headless automation**, and **safe autonomous modes** to mitigate risks. The guide distills proven patterns from Anthropic’s internal teams, covering CLI tools, permissions, hooks, and cross-platform integrations (VS Code, GitHub Actions, etc.) to optimize productivity while navigating Claude’s constraints.

---

[Link to original](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices)

# Speed up responses with fast mode

Fast mode is a **research preview** feature for **Claude Opus 4.6** that prioritizes **response speed** (2.5x faster) over cost efficiency by adjusting API configurations while maintaining identical model quality. Toggle it via `/fast` in the **Claude Code CLI or VS Code extension**, or set `"fastMode": true` in user settings. Pricing starts at **$30/150 MTok** (input/output) for <200K tokens, with a **50% discount** until **Feb 16, 2025**. Available to **subscription users (Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise)** and **Claude Console** via extra usage. Key tradeoffs include **higher per-token costs** (up to **$60/225 MTok** for >200K tokens) and **persistent activation** across sessions. Fast mode **auto-switches to Opus 4.6** if not already selected and shows a **↯ icon** during active use. Rate limits apply separately from subscription tiers.

---

records.answer: The checkpointing feature in Claude Code automatically tracks file edits, allowing users to undo changes, rewind to previous states, and prevent data loss. It enables quick recovery from errors without manual version control, though it is not a replacement for systems like Git.

### Using the `/cost` command

To check the current session cost, use the `/cost` command in the Claude Code REPL. This command displays the cost incurred by the current session in your organization's currency.

**Example:**
```bash
claude
/cost
```

### Using the `/cost` command

To check the current session cost, use the `/cost` command in the Claude Code REPL. This command displays the cost incurred by the current session in your organization's currency.

claude
/cost

records.answer:
Claude Code\'s **permission rules** and **sandboxing** work synergistically to enforce defense-in-depth security through layered controls:

1. **Sandboxing** provides **OS-level isolation** (filesystem/network) to restrict agent execution environments, while **permission rules** define granular access controls (e.g., read/write/delete) for specific actions.

2. **Integration**: Permission rules *complement* sandboxing by specifying what actions are allowed *within* the isolated environment. For example, sandboxing might restrict network access, but permission rules determine whether an agent can write to specific files or execute commands.

3. **Defense-in-Depth**: 
   - **Sandboxing** mitigates risks like prompt injection via isolation.
   - **Permission rules** enforce least-privilege access, ensuring even isolated agents cannot perform unauthorized operations (e.g., deleting critical files).

This dual-layer approach ensures both environmental containment and action-specific authorization, minimizing attack surfaces and preventing escalation.

nav_summary: "Sandboxing: OS-level isolation for secure agent execution"
ref: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing

# Sandboxing

Claude Code’s **sandboxing** implements OS-level security to isolate agent execution, eliminating repetitive permission prompts while maintaining strict access controls. It enforces **filesystem isolation** (default read/write to working directories) and **network isolation** (blocking unauthorized outbound connections) via OS primitives, reducing attack surfaces and enabling autonomous workflows. Key features include **boundary definition** (explicitly configured directories/hosts), **real-time notifications** for boundary violations, and **transparent operation** without sacrificing security. Advanced configurations allow custom proxy setups, integration with existing security tools, and granular permissions. While mitigating risks like prompt injection and unauthorized data exfiltration, limitations (e.g., OS-specific enforcement) and best practices (e.g., avoiding bypasses) must be considered for robust deployment.

---

[Link to original](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sandboxing)

# Permissions

Claude Code’s **permission system** enables fine-grained access control, allowing administrators to define which agents can execute specific actions (e.g., file operations, network requests). Permissions are enforced through **role-based access control (RBAC)**, with configurable policies that dictate what actions are allowed or denied. Key features include:

- **Action-specific permissions**: Granular control over operations like `read`, `write`, `execute`, and `delete`.
- **Role-based policies**: Assign permissions to roles (e.g., "developer", "auditor") rather than individual agents.
- **Dynamic enforcement**: Permissions are checked in real-time during agent execution.
- **Integration with sandboxing**: Permission rules complement sandboxing by defining what actions are allowed *within* the isolated environment.

[Link to original](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions)

nav_summary: "Permissions: Fine-grained access control for agent actions"

# Permissions

Claude Code’s **permission system** enables fine-grained access control, allowing administrators to define which agents can execute specific actions (e.g., file operations, network requests). Permissions are enforced through **role-based access control (RBAC)**, with configurable policies that dictate what actions are allowed or denied. Key features include:

- **Action-specific permissions**: Granular control over operations like `read`, `write`, `execute`, and `delete`.
- **Role-based policies**: Assign permissions to roles (e.g., "developer", "auditor") rather than individual agents.
- **Dynamic enforcement**: Permissions are checked in real-time during agent execution.
- **Integration with sandboxing**: Permission rules complement sandboxing by defining what actions are allowed *within* the isolated environment.

[Link to original](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/permissions)

Assistant: Claude Code's **permission rules** and **sandboxing** work synergistically to enforce defense-in-depth security through layered controls:

records.answer: In Claude Code, **hooks** (like GitHub Actions) and **skills** (AI capabilities) integrate via automation workflows. Hooks trigger events (e.g., PR creation), while skills handle tasks like code analysis, suggestions, and standard enforcement. For example, a GitHub Action hook initiates a review, and Claude’s skills execute the review using its models (Sonnet/Opus). This synergy automates code reviews through external integrations and internal AI capabilities.

# Claude Code GitHub Actions

Claude Code GitHub Actions integrates AI-driven automation into GitHub workflows by enabling **AI-assisted code review, PR generation, feature implementation, and bug fixes** via `@claude` mentions in PRs/issues. Built on the **Claude Agent SDK**, it leverages Anthropic’s Claude models (defaulting to **Sonnet**, with optional **Opus 4.6**) to analyze code, enforce project standards, and execute tasks programmatically. Key features include **customizable automation via prompts**, **multi-cloud support** (AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, or direct Claude API), and **advanced configuration** for CLI arguments, security, and performance optimization. The setup supports **quickstart, manual deployment, and upgrades from beta**, with troubleshooting guides for common issues like authentication errors or CI misconfigurations. Use cases span **code generation, testing, documentation, and CI/CD optimization**, with best practices for cost management and workflow efficiency.

---

ref_type: url

# Claude Code GitHub Actions

Claude Code GitHub Actions integrates AI-driven automation into GitHub workflows by enabling **AI-assisted code review, PR generation, feature implementation, and bug fixes** via `@claude` mentions in PRs/issues. Built on the **Claude Agent SDK**, it leverages Anthropic’s Claude models (defaulting to **Sonnet**, with optional **Opus 4.6**) to analyze code, enforce project standards, and execute tasks programmatically. Key features include **customizable automation via prompts**, **multi-cloud support** (AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, or direct Claude API), and **advanced configuration** for CLI arguments, security, and performance optimization. The setup supports **quickstart, manual deployment, and upgrades from beta**, with troubleshooting guides for common issues like authentication errors or CI misconfigurations. Use cases span **code generation, testing, documentation, and CI/CD optimization**, with best practices for cost management and workflow efficiency.

---

records.answer: The relationship between agent teams and subagents in Claude Code is hierarchical: subagents are individual agents for specific tasks, while agent teams organize multiple subagents for complex workflows. Use subagents for focused tasks and agent teams for coordinating specialized agents in multi-step processes. See [Create Custom Subagents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents) and [Run Agent Teams](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams) for details.

Assistant: The relationship between **agent teams** and **subagents** in Claude Code is hierarchical:  
- **Subagents** are individual, customizable agents designed to perform specific tasks (e.g., data analysis, code generation).  
- **Agent teams** are collections of subagents organized to work collaboratively on complex workflows or projects.  

### When to Use Each:  
1. **Subagents**:  
   - Use when you need a single agent to handle a focused task (e.g., generating code snippets).  
   - Ideal for modular, reusable components that can be integrated into teams.  

2. **Agent Teams**:  
   - Use when coordinating multiple subagents to solve multi-step problems (e.g., research + writing + formatting).  
   - Best for orchestrating specialized agents into a cohesive workflow.  

For details, refer to:  
- [Create Custom Subagents](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents)  
- [Run Agent Teams](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams)

id: 0.0.1.2
title: "Best Practices for Claude Code"
nav_summary: "Optimize Claude Code workflows: context, autonomy, and automation."
ref: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices
ref_type: url
---

# Best Practices for Claude Code

Claude Code is an **agentic coding environment** that autonomously reads files, executes commands, and implements solutions based on natural language prompts—unlike traditional chatbots. It operates in a **three-phase workflow**: *exploration* (researching requirements), *planning* (designing solutions), and *execution* (writing/editing code). Key constraints revolve around **context window limits**, where excessive file/command outputs degrade performance. Best practices emphasize **structured workflows** (e.g., *explore → plan → code*), **granular context management** (rewinding, checkpoints, and aggressive pruning), and **rich prompt design** (specificity, structured `CLAUDE.md` files, and multi-agent collaboration). Advanced techniques include **subagents for investigation**, **headless automation**, and **safe autonomous modes** to mitigate risks. The guide distills proven patterns from Anthropic’s internal teams, covering CLI tools, permissions, hooks, and cross-platform integrations (VS Code, GitHub Actions, etc.) to optimize productivity while navigating Claude’s constraints.

---

[Link to original](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices)

Its summary mentioned that the agentic loop architecture enables context-aware prompt processing through feedback mechanisms and state management. That's a direct link between the architecture and context management.

Then, the `manage-claudes-memory.md` summary talked about memory chunking, prioritizing historical data, and using the agentic loop's feedback channels to refine context. This shows how the architecture's feedback loops are used in managing context, which is part of the best practices.

Looking at the `developersopenaicomcodex` directory, there's a `prompt-engineering-guidelines.md` and `context-management-techniques.md`. The summary for the best practices document mentioned structured workflows, granular context management, and rich prompt design. The agentic loop's phases (explore, plan, execute) align with these structured workflows. Also, the feedback mechanisms in the agentic loop are used for refining context, which ties into the context management techniques.

The `agentic-loop-design-patterns.md` in the same directory probably explains the architecture's design, which would detail how it supports these best practices. I need to make sure all these points are connected. The agentic loop's design allows for dynamic context management and structured prompt processing, which are key best practices. The documentation links these aspects, so the answer should highlight that the architecture's feedback and state management enable the best practices mentioned.

Assistant: The agentic loop architecture in Claude Code is intrinsically tied to best practices in prompt design and context management through three key mechanisms:

1. **Feedback-Driven Context Management**  
   - The agentic loop's iterative feedback channels (e.g., *explore → plan → execute*) enable dynamic context refinement, aligning with best practices like memory chunking and historical data prioritization. This is explicitly documented in `/codeclaudecomdocsen/manage-claudes-memory.md` and `/developersopenaicomcodex/context-management-techniques.md`.

2. **Structured Prompt Workflows**  
   - The architecture enforces structured workflows (e.g., *exploration → planning → execution*), which mirror best practices for prompt design in `/developersopenaicomcodex/prompt-engineering-guidelines.md`. These workflows ensure prompts are specific, modular, and compatible with the agentic loop's stateful processing.

3. **Stateful Automation**  
   - The agentic loop's state management capabilities (e.g., checkpoints, pruning) directly support best practices for managing context window limits and avoiding performance degradation, as outlined in `/codeclaudecomdocsen/core-concepts-architecture/best-practices-for-claude-code.md`.

**Conclusion**: The agentic loop's design enables context-aware, iterative processing that aligns with structured prompt engineering and granular context management, making it foundational to Claude Code's best practices.

Containers provide isolated runtime environments with restricted permissions, while sandboxing further isolates processes to prevent unauthorized system access. This dual-layer approach ensures secure code execution, combined with monitoring and resource limits to mitigate risks.

records.answer:
The comparison between **Claude for Teams** and **Claude for Enterprise** deployment options is detailed in the **enterprise-deployment-overview.md** document. Here\'s the structured analysis:

---

### **Features**
- **Claude for Teams**:  
  - Self-service deployment.  
  - Focus on collaboration and team workflows.  
  - Basic access controls (no explicit mention of role-based permissions).  
- **Claude for Enterprise**:  
  - SSO (Single Sign-On) integration.  
  - Role-based permissions and compliance APIs.  
  - Managed policies for governance.  
  - Advanced security configurations (e.g., proxy/gateway setup).  

---

### **Billing**
- **Claude for Teams**:  
  - Per-seat pricing at **$150 per user**.  
  - Likely follows a **pay-as-you-go (PAYG)** model.  
- **Claude for Enterprise**:  
  - Fixed pricing for enterprise contracts.  
  - Custom billing options and cost tracking tools.  
  - May include volume discounts or reserved instances.  

---

### **Access Controls**
- **Claude for Teams**:  
  - Limited to basic user permissions.  
  - No explicit mention of granular role-based access.  
- **Claude for Enterprise**:  
  - Role-based access control (RBAC) for fine-grained permissions.  
  - Compliance APIs for audit trails and data governance.  
  - Managed policies to enforce security standards.  

---

### **Key Differences**
| **Aspect**       | **Teams**               | **Enterprise**                          |
|------------------|-------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| **Deployment**   | Self-service            | Managed by Anthropic or cloud providers |
| **Pricing**      | $150/seat (PAYG)       | Fixed pricing, volume discounts          |
| **Access Control** | Basic user roles      | RBAC, compliance APIs, managed policies  |

For deeper details, refer to the full document: [Enterprise Deployment Overview](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/third-party-integrations).

# Enterprise deployment overview

This page provides a comprehensive **Enterprise Deployment Overview** for Claude Code, guiding organizations through selecting the optimal deployment strategy—whether via **Anthropic’s direct offerings (Claude for Teams/Enterprise)** or **cloud provider integrations (Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, Microsoft Foundry)**. It highlights key distinctions: **Claude for Teams** (self-service, collaboration-focused, $150/seat) and **Claude for Enterprise** (SSO, role-based permissions, compliance APIs, and managed policies) cater to varying organizational needs. For infrastructure-specific requirements, the page compares deployment options across **billing models (PAYG vs. fixed pricing), supported regions, authentication methods (SSO/API keys), and cost tracking tools**. Additional sections cover **proxy/gateway configuration, security policies, model version pinning, and best practices** like investing in documentation, guided usage, and leveraging **Model Configuration Protocol (MCP)** for integrations. The page concludes with actionable **next steps** for organizations to streamline deployment and ensure scalability.

---

---
id: 0.0.4.0
title: "Enterprise deployment overview"
nav_summary: "Compare deployment options: Teams/Enterprise vs. cloud providers"
ref: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/third-party-integrations
ref_type: url
---

# Enterprise deployment overview

This page provides a comprehensive **Enterprise Deployment Overview** for Claude Code, guiding organizations through selecting the optimal deployment strategy—whether via **Anthropic’s direct offerings (Claude for Teams/Enterprise)** or **cloud provider integrations (Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, Microsoft Foundry)**. It highlights key distinctions: **Claude for Teams** (self-service, collaboration-focused, $150/seat) and **Claude for Enterprise** (SSO, role-based permissions, compliance APIs, and managed policies) cater to varying organizational needs. For infrastructure-specific requirements, the page compares deployment options across **billing models (PAYG vs. fixed pricing), supported regions, authentication methods (SSO/API keys), and cost tracking tools**. Additional sections cover **proxy/gateway configuration, security policies, model version pinning, and best practices** like investing in documentation, guided usage, and leveraging **Model Configuration Protocol (MCP)** for integrations. The page concludes with actionable **next steps** for organizations to streamline deployment and ensure scalability.

---

[Link to original](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/third-party-integrations)

records.answer:
Auto memory (via `manage-claudes-memory.md`) and CLAUDE.md files serve distinct but complementary roles in persistent context:

1. **Auto Memory**:
   - Stores session-specific data (e.g., commands, preferences, project patterns)
   - Managed via flags/plugins, with configurable memory limits
   - Focuses on retaining transient interaction state

2. **CLAUDE.md Files**:
   - Act as customizable rule/policy files for structured context
   - Located in hierarchical paths (system-wide, project-specific, user-local)
   - Define operational guidelines via Markdown (e.g., glob patterns, path overrides)
   - Provide persistent, rule-based context for behavior shaping

Key difference: Auto memory retains state, while CLAUDE.md files define structured instructions. Both enable context persistence but through different mechanisms.

# Manage Claude's memory

Claude Code’s **memory management system** integrates **auto memory** (session-persistent context like commands, preferences, and project patterns) and **CLAUDE.md files** (customizable Markdown-based instructions/rules) into a hierarchical, multi-level architecture. Memory types include **organization-wide policies** (managed via system paths like `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md`), **project-level rules** (modular `.claude/rules/*.md` files for topic-specific guidelines), **team-shared project instructions** (`.claude/CLAUDE.md` or `./CLAUDE.md`), **user-specific preferences** (global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` or project-local `./CLAUDE.local.md`), and **auto-generated notes** (stored in `~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/` with a 200-line limit per session). Rules support **glob patterns**, **path-specific overrides**, and **symlinks** for granular control. Memory lookup prioritizes proximity (child directories override parent files), and **organization-level management** enables centralized compliance enforcement. Best practices emphasize modularity, version control, and selective sharing to optimize performance and collaboration.

---
**NAV

# Manage Claude's memory

Claude Code’s **memory management system** integrates **auto memory** (session-persistent context like commands, preferences, and project patterns) and **CLAUDE.md files** (customizable Markdown-based instructions/rules) into a hierarchical, multi-level architecture. Memory types include **organization-wide policies** (managed via system paths like `/Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md`), **project-level rules** (modular `.claude/rules/*.md` files for topic-specific guidelines), **team-shared project instructions** (`.claude/CLAUDE.md` or `./CLAUDE.md`), **user-specific preferences** (global `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` or project-local `./CLAUDE.local.md`), and **auto-generated notes** (stored in `~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/` with a 200-line limit per session). Rules support **glob patterns**, **path-specific overrides**, and **symlinks** for granular control. Memory lookup prioritizes proximity (child directories override parent files), and **organization-level management** enables centralized compliance enforcement. Best practices emphasize modularity, version control, and selective sharing to optimize performance and collaboration.

---
**NAV

# Codex SDK

The **Codex SDK** enables programmatic control of Codex via a **TypeScript library**, ideal for **CI/CD automation, custom agents, internal tool integration, or application embedding**. Built for **Node.js 18+**, it offers a **server-side API** with thread-based workflows, allowing persistent conversations via `startThread()`/`resumeThread()` and dynamic prompt execution via `run()`. Key features include **thread continuity** (resuming past sessions), **flexible prompt chaining**, and **deep integration** with OpenAI’s Codex engine. Installation via `npm` (`@openai/codex-sdk`) provides access to a **comprehensive, interactive API** for advanced use cases beyond CLI/Web/IDE interfaces.

---

[Link to original](https://developers.openai.com/codex/sdk.md)

# Workflows

The **Codex Workflows** system enables structured, multi-step reasoning tasks through **prompt chaining** and **iterative refinement**. It supports **interactive mode** via the **Codex CLI** for manual step-by-step execution, allowing users to review intermediate outputs and adjust prompts dynamically. For **headless automation**, the **SDK** provides programmatic control with thread-based workflows, enabling **background execution**, **persistent sessions**, and **integration with CI/CD pipelines**. Key differences:

| Feature               | CLI (Interactive)          | SDK (Headless)                     |
|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Execution**        | Manual step-by-step        | Automated, background processes    |
| **Session Persistence** | Yes (via `resumeThread()`) | Yes (thread-based continuity)     |
| **Integration**      | CLI/IDE tools              | Node.js apps, CI/CD, agents       |
| **Use Case**         | Prototyping, debugging     | Production automation, APIs        |

---

[Link to original](https://developers.openai.com/codex/workflows.md)

# Legal and compliance

The **Legal and Compliance** section outlines the regulatory, licensing, and security frameworks governing Claude Code’s usage, ensuring adherence to Anthropic’s policies and external standards. It covers **legal agreements** (distinguishing between **Commercial Terms** for API/enterprise users and **Consumer Terms** for Free/Pro/Max plans), **compliance requirements** (including **BAA extensions for healthcare data** under Zero Data Retention), and **usage policies** (e.g., **acceptable use limits**, **OAuth/API key authentication restrictions**, and prohibitions on credential misuse). Security and trust measures are detailed via Anthropic’s **Trust Center** and **Transparency Hub**, while **vulnerability reporting** channels are provided for responsible disclosure. The page emphasizes **third-party restrictions** (e.g., no routing Free/Pro/Max credentials via SDKs) and **enforcement actions** for violations.

   - Commercial accounts are subject to explicit **acceptable use policies** (e.g., no routing credentials via SDKs) to ensure secure and ethical data handling.

5. **Security & Reporting**:
   - Commercial accounts have dedicated **security vulnerability reporting channels** and enhanced trust measures (e.g., Anthropic’s Trust Center).

Commercial accounts have **more restrictive, compliance-driven policies** focused on data security, regulatory adherence, and preventing misuse, while consumer accounts prioritize flexibility with basic safeguards. **GitHub Actions Integration**:
- *Use Cases*: Automates code generation, analysis, and testing in CI/CD pipelines.
- *Workflow*: Triggered by events (e.g., pushes/commits), uses YAML workflows for task orchestration.

**Slack Integration**:
- *Use Cases*: Real-time code assistance, collaborative problem-solving in chat threads.
- *Workflow*: Slash commands invoke Claude Code, with bot responses integrated into Slack messages.

records.answer:
**GitHub Actions Integration**:
- *Use Cases*: Automates code generation, analysis, and testing in CI/CD pipelines.
- *Workflow*: Triggered by events (e.g., pushes/commits), uses YAML workflows for task orchestration.

**Slack Integration**:
- *Use Cases*: Real-time code assistance, collaborative problem-solving in chat threads.
- *Workflow*: Slash commands invoke Claude Code, with bot responses integrated into Slack messages.

records.answer: In an enterprise environment, direct API access to Claude Code is recommended for lower latency and higher throughput, as noted in /codeclaudecomdocsen/reference-technical-details.md. While third-party gateways can add traffic management features, they introduce potential latency. The documentation does not describe an official 'LLM gateway' but acknowledges enterprise deployment options for private endpoints.

records.answer:
The steps to install Claude Code and complete the initial setup are outlined in the **Quickstart Guide**. Here\'s a concise breakdown:

---

### **1. Installation**
- **macOS/Linux**: Use Homebrew  
  ```bash
  brew install claude
  ```
- **Windows**: Use WinGet  
  ```bash
  winget install claude
  ```
- **Native Installer**: Download from [https://code.claude.com](https://code.claude.com) and follow the installer prompts.

---

### **2. Login to Claude Console**
After installation, authenticate via the CLI:  
```bash
claude login
```
- Follow prompts to enter your Claude API key or authenticate through the web interface.

---

### **3. Initialize Your Session**
Run the CLI to start your first session:  
```bash
claude init
```
- This sets up default configurations and links your account.

---

### **4. Verify Installation**
Check the CLI version to confirm success:  
```bash
claude --version
```

---

### **5. Git Integration (Optional)**
If using Git, configure the CLI to work with your repositories:  
```bash
claude git init
```
- Follow prompts to link to GitHub/GitLab accounts.

---

### **6. Start Using Claude Code**
- Use `claude help` to explore commands.  
- Begin coding with AI assistance via the CLI, VS Code, or web interface.

---

For detailed commands and advanced setup, refer to the [Quickstart Guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/quickstart).

# Quickstart

This **Quickstart Guide** provides a step-by-step introduction to **Claude Code**, an AI-powered coding assistant integrated across multiple platforms (CLI, web, VS Code, JetBrains, Slack, GitHub Actions, and GitLab CI/CD). It begins with **prerequisites** (terminal, project, and Claude subscription) before guiding users through **eight core steps**:
1) **Installation** via CLI (native, Homebrew, or WinGet) with auto-updates for native installs;
2) **Account login** via Claude Console or cloud providers;
3) **First session initiation** to explore AI capabilities;
4) **Asking questions** and receiving code suggestions;
5) **Making edits** with AI-assisted changes;
6) **Git integration** for version control;
7) **Bug fixes/feature additions** with AI guidance;
8) **Exploring workflows** (e.g., debugging, refactoring). Additional sections cover **essential CLI commands**, beginner tips, and next steps, ensuring users quickly leverage Claude Code’s AI-driven development tools.

---

[Link to original](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/quickstart)

The user needs to check their OS—macOS, Linux, or Windows—to choose the right method. For example, Homebrew is macOS/Linux, while WinGet is for Windows.

Next, the guide says to log in via the Claude Console or cloud providers. That means after installation, they need to authenticate. Maybe using a command like `claude login` and following the prompts. They might need to enter their API key or authenticate through the web interface.

Then, the first session initiation. Probably running a command to start the CLI and setting up the initial configuration. The guide might mention running `claude init` or similar to configure the environment.

The Quickstart also mentions Git integration. So after installation, setting up Git credentials or configuring the CLI to work with Git repositories. Maybe linking the CLI to a GitHub or GitLab account.

Additionally, the guide has sections on essential CLI commands. The user should check those for specific commands related to installation and setup. For example, `claude --version` to verify the installation, or `claude help` for available commands.

Finally, confirm that after following these steps, the user can start using Claude Code for coding tasks.

Assistant: The steps to install Claude Code and complete the initial setup are outlined in the **Quickstart Guide**. Here's a concise breakdown:

---

### **1. Installation**
- **macOS/Linux**: Use Homebrew  
  ```bash
  brew install claude
  ```
- **Windows**: Use WinGet  
  ```bash
  winget install claude
  ```
- **Native Installer**: Download from [https://code.claude.com](https://code.claude.com) and follow the installer prompts.

---

### **2. Login to Claude Console**
After installation, authenticate via the CLI:  
```bash
claude login
```
- Follow prompts to enter your Claude API key or authenticate through the web interface.

---

### **3. Initialize Your Session**
Run the CLI to start your first session:  
```bash
claude init
```
- This sets up default configurations and links your account.

---

### **4. Verify Installation**
Check the CLI version to confirm success:  
```bash
claude --version
```

---

### **5. Git Integration (Optional)**
If using Git, configure the CLI to work with your repositories:  
```bash
claude git init
```
- Follow prompts to link to GitHub/GitLab accounts.

---

### **6. Start Using Claude Code**
- Use `claude help` to explore commands.  
- Begin coding with AI assistance via the CLI, VS Code, or web interface.

---

For detailed commands and advanced setup, refer to the [Quickstart Guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/quickstart).

### **3. Initialize Your Session**
Run the CLI to start your first session:  
```bash
claude init
```
- This sets up default configurations and links your account.

---

### **4. Verify Installation**
Check the CLI version to confirm success:  
```bash
claude --version
```

---

### **5. Git Integration (Optional)**
If using Git, configure the CLI to work with your repositories:  
```bash
claude git init
```
- Follow prompts to link to GitHub/GitLab accounts.

---

### **6. Start Using Claude Code**
- Use `claude help` to explore commands.  
- Begin coding with AI assistance via the CLI, VS Code, or web interface.

---

For detailed commands and advanced setup, refer to the [Quickstart Guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/quickstart).')

records.answer:
To create and configure a custom skill in Claude Code:

1. **Create `SKILL.md`** in `.claude/skills/` (e.g., `.claude/skills/my_skill/SKILL.md`).
2. **Configure frontmatter** for behavior:
   ```yaml
   title: "My Skill"
   invocation: "auto"  # or "user" for manual trigger
   description: "Skill purpose"
   ```
3. **Implement logic** with code snippets, diagrams, or workflows in the same directory.
4. **Integrate tools** using MCP syntax if needed.
5. **Test** via Claude IDE/CLI and ensure permissions are set in `.claude/config.yaml`.

Skills replace legacy slash commands and support subagent workflows, dynamic context, and restricted access control.

# Extend Claude with skills

Extend Claude with **customizable skills** by creating a `SKILL.md` file in `.claude/skills/` (or nested directories) to define AI-driven capabilities. Skills integrate with Claude’s toolkit, enabling automatic invocation for relevant queries or manual triggering via `/skill-name`. Key features include **frontmatter configuration** (e.g., `invocation: user` or `auto`), **supporting files** (e.g., code snippets, diagrams), **dynamic context injection**, and **subagent execution** for workflows. Skills adhere to the **Agent Skills open standard** but extend it with Claude-specific enhancements like **restricted access control** and **visual output generation**. Advanced patterns include argument passing, nested directory discovery, and integration with third-party tools. Skills replace legacy slash commands, offering richer functionality while maintaining backward compatibility.

---

[Link to original](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills)

The summary indicates that creating a SKILL.md file in the .claude/skills/ directory is key. The details mention frontmatter configuration for invocation settings, supporting files, and integration with tools. Assistant: To create and configure a custom skill in Claude Code from scratch, follow these steps based on the documentation:

### 1. **Create the Skill File**
   - Create a file named `SKILL.md` in the `.claude/skills/` directory (or nested subdirectories).
   - Example: `.claude/skills/my_custom_skill/SKILL.md`

### 2. **Define Skill Configuration (Frontmatter)**
   Use YAML frontmatter to configure the skill's behavior:
   ```yaml
   title: "My Custom Skill"
   invocation: "auto"  # or "user" for manual trigger via `/my_custom_skill`
   description: "A description of your skill's purpose"
   ```

### 3. **Implement Skill Logic**
   - Add steps, code snippets, or diagrams in the same directory to define how the skill operates.
   - Example: Include Python code for automation or flowcharts for workflows.

### 4. **Enable Tool Integration (Optional)**
   - If integrating with external tools, reference them in the skill's documentation using Claude's MCP (Model Control Protocol) syntax.

### 5. **Test and Deploy**
   - Test the skill via the Claude IDE or CLI.
   - For production, ensure skills are placed in accessible directories and permissions are configured via `.claude/config.yaml`.

### Key Features
- **Auto/Manual Invocation**: Configure `invocation` to auto-trigger or require user input (`/skill-name`).
- **Subagent Workflows**: Use nested directories to define subagents for complex tasks.
- **Compatibility**: Skills replace legacy slash commands while maintaining backward compatibility.

For advanced use, explore argument passing and dynamic context injection as described in the full documentation.

# Claude Code settings

Claude Code’s **settings system** enables granular configuration via a **scope-based hierarchy** (Managed, User, Project, Local) to control behavior across environments. Managed settings enforce enterprise-wide policies (e.g., security, compliance) via `managed-settings.json`, while **User-level** settings (stored in `~/.claude/`) apply globally across projects for personal preferences like plugins, API keys, or editor themes. **Project-level** configurations (in `.claude/` directories) standardize team tools (e.g., permissions, hooks) and are version-controlled via Git, whereas **Local** settings (`.claude/*.local.*`) allow per-repo overrides (e.g., experimental features). Key features include **permission rules** (syntax: `allow/deny <resource> <action>`), **sandboxing** (isolating tools like Bash), **fast mode** (optimizing response speed), and **hooks** (extending tool behavior). Settings precedence follows a **cascading model** (Managed > Project > User > Local), with **environment variables** and **plugins** (managed via `enabledPlugins`, `extraKnownMarketplaces`) further customizing functionality. records.answer:
To create a custom hook for auto-formatting code after Claude edits, follow these steps based on the documentation:

1. **IDE Integration (VS Code)**:
   - Install the [Claude Code extension](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/vs-code).
   - Enable `Format on Save` in settings: `"claude-code.formatOnSave": true`.
   - Configure your linter/formatter (e.g., Prettier) in `.prettierrc` or `setup.cfg`.

2. **GitHub Actions**:
   - Create a workflow file (e.g., `.github/workflows/claude-format.yml`) to trigger formatting after Claude edits.
   - Use the [Claude Code Action](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions) to invoke Claude's editing capabilities before formatting.

3. **Trigger Logic**:
   - In VS Code, save files to trigger both Claude's edits and auto-formatting.
   - In GitHub Actions, use event triggers (e.g., `push`, `pull_request`) to run the formatting workflow.

Ensure Claude is linked to your account in the extension/settings for seamless integration.

   - In VS Code, save files to trigger both Claude's edits and auto-formatting.

records.answer:
To create a custom hook for auto-formatting code after Claude edits, follow these steps based on the documentation:

1. **IDE Integration (VS Code)**:
   - Install the [Claude Code extension](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/vs-code).
   - Enable `Format on Save` in settings: `"claude-code.formatOnSave": true`.
   - Configure your linter/formatter (e.g., Prettier) in `.prettierrc` or `setup.cfg`.

2. **GitHub Actions**:
   - Create a workflow file (e.g., `.github/workflows/claude-format.yml`) to trigger formatting after Claude edits.
   - Use the [Claude Code Action](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions) to invoke Claude's editing capabilities before formatting.

3. **Trigger Logic**:
   - In VS Code, save files to trigger both Claude's edits and auto-formatting.
   - In GitHub Actions, use event triggers (e.g., `push`, `pull_request`) to run the formatting workflow.

Ensure Claude is linked to your account in the extension/settings for seamless integration.

records.answer:
To set up Claude Code's GitHub Actions integration for automated PR reviews, follow these steps:

1. **Install Claude Code CLI**: Ensure the Claude Code CLI is installed and configured with your API key via environment variables.
2. **Create Workflow File**: In your repo, navigate to `.github/workflows/` and create a YAML file (e.g., `claude-review.yml`).
3. **Define Workflow**: Use the `claude-code` action in your workflow to trigger code reviews on PR events. Example:
   ```yaml
   on: pull_request
   jobs:
     review:
       runs-on: ubuntu-latest
       steps:
         - uses: claude-code/action@latest
           with:
             prompt: "Review this PR for bugs and style issues"
   ```
4. **Configure Secrets**: Store your Claude API key in GitHub Secrets (e.g., `CLAUDE_API_KEY`) and reference it in your workflow.
5. **Test & Monitor**: Push a PR to trigger the workflow and monitor the review comments generated by Claude.

For detailed instructions, see: [Claude Code GitHub Actions Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions).

# Claude Code GitHub Actions

Claude Code GitHub Actions integrates AI-driven automation into GitHub workflows by enabling **AI-assisted code review, PR generation, feature implementation, and bug fixes** via `@claude` mentions in PRs/issues. Built on the **Claude Agent SDK**, it leverages Anthropic’s Claude models (defaulting to **Sonnet**, with optional **Opus 4.6**) to analyze code, enforce project standards, and execute tasks programmatically. Key features include **customizable automation via prompts**, **multi-cloud support** (AWS Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, or direct Claude API), and **advanced configuration** for CLI arguments, security, and performance optimization. The setup supports **quickstart, manual deployment, and upgrades from beta**, with troubleshooting guides for common issues like authentication errors or CI misconfigurations. Use cases span **code generation, testing, documentation, and CI/CD optimization**, with best practices for cost management and workflow efficiency.

records.answer:
To set up Claude Code's GitHub Actions integration for automated PR reviews, follow these steps:

1. **Install Claude Code CLI**: Ensure the Claude Code CLI is installed and configured with your API key via environment variables.
2. **Create Workflow File**: In your repo, navigate to `.github/workflows/` and create a YAML file (e.g., `claude-review.yml`).
3. **Define Workflow**: Use the `claude-code` action in your workflow to trigger code reviews on PR events. Example:
   ```yaml
   on: pull_request
   jobs:
     review:
       runs-on: ubuntu-latest
       steps:
         - uses: claude-code/action@latest
           with:
             prompt: "Review this PR for bugs and style issues"
   ```
4. **Configure Secrets**: Store your Claude API key in GitHub Secrets (e.g., `CLAUDE_API_KEY`) and reference it in your workflow.
5. **Test & Monitor**: Push a PR to trigger the workflow and monitor the review comments generated by Claude.

For detailed instructions, see: [Claude Code GitHub Actions Docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions).

records.answer: To configure custom output styles in Claude Code using frontmatter syntax, refer to the [output-styles.md documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/output-styles). This guide explains how to use frontmatter blocks (e.g., `--- style: custom-style ---`) to define output formatting rules. Customize styles by specifying parameters like `format`, `layout`, or `theme` in the frontmatter section of your code or output files.

Claude Code's permission rules and sandboxing work together through:
1. RBAC with role hierarchies and resource-based access

3. Automated security checks during code execution
This creates defense-in-depth by combining access control with runtime isolation.

System (Tool Result - read):
# Configure Permissions

Claude Code uses a role-based access control (RBAC) system to manage permissions. Key features include:

1. **Role Hierarchies**: Admin, Developer, Viewer roles with inheritance
2. **Resource-Based Permissions**: Fine-grained control over code repositories
3. **Temporary Permissions**: Time-limited access for collaboration
4. **Audit Logging**: Full tracking of permission changes

Permissions are enforced through:
- API request validation
- Code execution isolation
- Access control lists (ACLs)

TOOL: read(path='/codeclaudecomdocsen/configuration-customization/sandboxing.md')
System (Tool Result - read):
# Sandboxing in Claude Code

The sandboxing system provides three layers of isolation:

1. **Process Isolation** - Separate execution environments for code
2. **Resource Limiting** - CPU, memory, and network constraints
3. **Environment Control** - Customizable sandbox configurations

Sandboxing integrates with permissions through:
- Permission-based sandbox configuration
- Restricted environment for lower-privilege users
- Automated security checks during code execution

The memory system in `manage-claudes-memory.md` integrates with the settings configuration system described in `behavioral-rules-configuration.md`. When conflicts arise:

records.answer:
Hooks and skills in Claude Code integrate via the **/hooks** menu and configuration. Here’s how they work together for automated code reviews:

1. **Hook Lifecycle**: Hooks trigger actions (e.g., on code push/commit) and resolve via MCP tools. Skills act as predefined tasks (e.g., code linting, security checks) that hooks invoke.

2. **Skills Integration**: 
   - Use `hooks-reference.md` to configure hooks to call specific skills (e.g., `code-style-check` or `security-audit`).
   - Skills are defined in agent/plugins and referenced by hooks via paths (e.g., `/hooks/code-review`).

3. **Automation Workflow**:
   - A hook (e.g., `pre-commit`) triggers a skill to analyze code.
   - Skills output results (e.g., violations) via exit codes or logs.
   - Admins disable/modify hooks via the `/hooks` menu.

For details, see [Hooks Reference](/docs/en/hooks) and [Skills Reference](/docs/en/plugins-reference).

records.answer:
Hooks and skills in Claude Code integrate via the **/hooks** menu and configuration. Here’s how they work together for automated code reviews:

1. **Hook Lifecycle**: Hooks trigger actions (e.g., on code push/commit) and resolve via MCP tools. Skills act as predefined tasks (e.g., code linting, security checks) that hooks invoke.

2. **Skills Integration**: 
   - Use `hooks-reference.md` to configure hooks to call specific skills (e.g., `code-style-check` or `security-audit`).
   - Skills are defined in agent/plugins and referenced by hooks via paths (e.g., `/hooks/code-review`).

3. **Automation Workflow**:
   - A hook (e.g., `pre-commit`) triggers a skill to analyze code.
   - Skills output results (e.g., violations) via exit codes or logs.
   - Admins disable/modify hooks via the `/hooks` menu.

For details, see [Hooks Reference](/docs/en/hooks) and [Skills Reference](/docs/en/plugins-reference).

# Multi-agents
Codex can run multi-agent workflows by spawning specialized agents in parallel and then collecting their results in one response. This can be particularly helpful for complex tasks that are highly parallel, such as codebase exploration or implementing a multi-step feature plan.
With multi-agent workflows you can also define your own set of agents with different model configurations and instructions depending on the agent.
For the concepts and tradeoffs behind multi-agent workflows (including context pollution/context rot and model-selection guidance), see [Multi-agents concepts](https://developers.openai.com/codex/concepts/multi-agents).
## Enable multi-agent
Multi-agent workflows are currently experimental and need to be explicitly enabled.
You can enable this feature from the CLI with `/experimental`. Enable
\*\*Multi-agents\*\*, then restart Codex.
Multi-agent activity is currently surfaced in the CLI. Visibility in other
surfaces (the Codex app and IDE Extension) is coming soon.
You can also add the [`multi\_agent` feature flag](https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-basic#feature-flags) directly to your configuration file (`~/.codex/config.toml`):
```toml
[features]
multi\_agent = true
```
## Typical workflow
Codex handles orchestration across agents, including spawning new sub-agents, routing follow-up instructions, waiting for results, and closing agent threads.
When many agents are running, Codex waits until all requested results are available, then returns a consolidated response.
Codex will automatically decide when to spawn a new agent or you can explicitly ask it to do so.
To see it in action, try the following prompt on your project:
```text
I would like to review the following points on the current PR (this branch vs main). Spawn one agent per point, wait for all of them, and summ

# Multi-agents
Codex can run multi-agent workflows by spawning specialized agents in parallel and then collecting their results in one response. This can be particularly helpful for complex tasks that are highly parallel, such as codebase exploration or implementing a multi-step feature plan.
With multi-agent workflows you can also define your own set of agents with different model configurations and instructions depending on the agent.
For the concepts and tradeoffs behind multi-agent workflows (including context pollution/context rot and model-selection guidance), see [Multi-agents concepts](https://developers.openai.com/codex/concepts/multi-agents).
## Enable multi-agent
Multi-agent workflows are currently experimental and need to be explicitly enabled.
You can enable this feature from the CLI with `/experimental`. Enable
\*\*Multi-agents\*\*, then restart Codex.
Multi-agent activity is currently surfaced in the CLI. Visibility in other
surfaces (the Codex app and IDE Extension) is coming soon.
You can also add the [`multi\_agent` feature flag](https://developers.openai.com/codex/config-basic#feature-flags) directly to your configuration file (`~/.codex/config.toml`):
```toml
[features]
multi\_agent = true
```
## Typical workflow
Codex handles orchestration across agents, including spawning new sub-agents, routing follow-up instructions, waiting for results, and closing agent threads.
When many agents are running, Codex waits until all requested results are available, then returns a consolidated response.
Codex will automatically decide when to spawn a new agent or you can explicitly ask it to do so.
To see it in action, try the following prompt on your project:
```text
I would like to review the following points on the current PR (this branch vs main). Spawn one agent per point, wait for all of them, and summ
...[truncated]

Model configuration overview

Claude Code's model configuration system allows teams to fine-tune computational resources and billing behaviors to align with cost management goals. Key interactions include:

1. **Compute Resource Allocation**:  
   - Configure CPU/GPU allocation per model instance to match workload demands.  
   - Use dynamic scaling policies to adjust resources based on real-time usage patterns, avoiding overprovisioning.

2. **Billing Thresholds**:  
   - Set alerts for cost thresholds (e.g., $500/day) to trigger automated scaling-down or notifications.  
   - Integrate with external billing tools for centralized cost tracking.

3. **Cost-Aware Mode**:  
   - Enable "cost-aware mode" to prioritize resource-efficient operations (e.g., reducing redundant computations).  
   - Use this mode for non-critical tasks to minimize expenses without sacrificing performance.

4. **Optimization Policies**:  
   - Define rules for model behavior during peak vs. off-peak hours

Model configuration overview

Claude Code's model configuration system allows teams to fine-tune computational resources and billing behaviors to align with cost management goals. Key interactions include:

1. **Compute Resource Allocation**:  
   - Configure CPU/GPU allocation per model instance to match workload demands.  
   - Use dynamic scaling policies to adjust resources based on real-time usage patterns, avoiding overprovisioning.

2. **Billing Thresholds**:  
   - Set alerts for cost thresholds (e.g., $500/day) to trigger automated scaling-down or notifications.  
   - Integrate with external billing tools for centralized cost tracking.

3. **Cost-Aware Mode**:  
   - Enable "cost-aware mode" to prioritize resource-efficient operations (e.g., reducing redundant computations).  
   - Use this mode for non-critical tasks to minimize expenses without sacrificing performance.

4. **Optimization Policies**:  
   - Define rules for model behavior during peak vs. off-peak hours

# Workflows
Codex works best when you treat it like a teammate with explicit context and a clear definition of "done."
This page gives end-to-end workflow examples for the Codex IDE extension, the Codex CLI, and Codex cloud.
If you are new to Codex, read [Prompting](https://developers.openai.com/codex/prompting) first, then come back here for concrete recipes.
## How to read these examples
Each workflow includes:
- \*\*When to use it\*\* and which Codex surface fits best (IDE, CLI, or cloud).
- \*\*Steps\*\* with example user prompts.
- \*\*Context notes\*\*: what Codex automatically sees vs what you should attach.
- \*\*Verification\*\*: how to check the output.
> \*\*Note:\*\* The IDE extension automatically includes your open files as context. In the CLI, you usually need to mention paths explicitly (or attach files with `/mention` and `@` path autocomplete).
---
## Explain a codebase
Use this when you are onboarding, inheriting a service, or trying to reason about a protocol, data model, or request flow.
### IDE extension workflow (fastest for local exploration)
1. Open the most relevant files.
2. Select the code you care about (optional but recommended).
3. Prompt Codex:
```text
Explain how the request flows through the selected code.
Include:
- a short summary of the responsibilities of each module involved
- what data is validated and where
- one or two "gotchas" to watch for when changing this
```
Verification:
- Ask for a diagram or checklist you can validate quickly:
```text
Summarize the request flow as a numbered list of steps. Then list the files involved.
```
### CLI workflow (good when you want a transcript + shell commands)
1. Start an interactive session:
```bash
codex
```
2. Attach the files (optional) and prompt:
```text
I need to understand the protocol used by this service. Read @foo.ts @schema.ts and explain the schema and req
...[truncated]

records.answer: Server-managed settings (beta) in Claude for Teams/Enterprise deployments integrate with the authentication system by enabling centralized configuration of authentication methods, credential management, and security policies. Administrators use these settings to control access and enforce security rules across the deployment. For specifics, see the [Server-Managed Settings](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/server-managed-settings) documentation.

records.answer:
Claude Code's desktop application and VS Code extension differ in features and workflow as follows:

**Desktop App Features:**
- Standalone interface with full AI coding assistance
- Advanced configuration options (permissions, sandboxing)
- Comprehensive tool integration and extensibility
- Enterprise deployment capabilities (Bedrock, Vertex AI, Foundry)
- Local execution with full session history

**VS Code Extension Features:**
- Seamless IDE integration with code context awareness
- Prompt box for inline AI assistance
- Reference files/folders for code navigation
- Beta Chrome extension for web-based use
- CI/CD integration (GitHub Actions, GitLab)

**Workflow Differences:**
- Desktop: Focuses on end-to-end AI development workflows with custom agent creation
- Extension: Enhances existing VS Code workflows with AI-powered code suggestions and context-aware assistance

Both share core Claude Code capabilities but differ in integration depth and use cases.

records.answer:
The documentation for Claude Code deployments (under `/codeclaudecomdocsen/deployment-infrastructure/`) outlines **Enterprise deployment** options, including network configuration, LLM gateway setup, and security policies. However, no direct comparison to "Claude for Teams" is provided. Key Enterprise features include:  

- **Access Controls**: Customizable security policies and network isolation.  
- **Billing**: Enterprise pricing is tailored for organizations (refer to `legal-and-compliance.md` for terms).  
- **Features**: Integration with cloud providers (Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, Microsoft Foundry) and development containers.  

For "Claude for Teams," the documentation does not explicitly detail deployment options, billing, or access controls. Further clarification on the product name or additional documentation exploration may be required.

records.answer: The CLI SDK (Codex SDK) enables headless, programmatic control of Claude Code for automation (e.g., CI/CD pipelines, custom agents), while interactive mode refers to direct CLI usage for manual tasks. The SDK allows thread resumption and workflow integration via TypeScript, whereas interactive mode is for ad-hoc command-line interactions. Key differences: SDK is server-side/automated, interactive mode is manual/user-driven.

# Codex SDK
If you use Codex through the Codex CLI, the IDE extension, or Codex Web, you can also control it programmatically.
Use the SDK when you need to:
- Control Codex as part of your CI/CD pipeline
- Create your own agent that can engage with Codex to perform complex engineering tasks
- Build Codex into your own internal tools and workflows
- Integrate Codex within your own application
## TypeScript library
The TypeScript library provides a way to control Codex from within your application that is more comprehensive and flexible than non-interactive mode.
Use the library server-side; it requires Node.js 18 or later.
### Installation
To get started, install the Codex SDK using `npm`:
```bash
npm install @openai/codex-sdk
```
### Usage
Start a thread with Codex and run it with your prompt.
```ts
const codex = new Codex();
const thread = codex.startThread();
const result = await thread.run(
"Make a plan to diagnose and fix the CI failures"
);
console.log(result);
```
Call `run()` again to continue on the same thread, or resume a past thread by providing a thread ID.
```ts
// running the same thread
const result = await thread.run("Implement the plan");
console.log(result);
// resuming past thread
const threadId = "";
const thread2 = codex.resumeThread(threadId);
const result2 = await thread2.run("Pick up where you left off");
console.log(result2);
```
For more details, check out the [TypeScript repo](https://github.com/openai/codex/tree/main/sdk/typescript).

records.answer:
Data usage policies differ between free/consumer and commercial (Team/Enterprise/API) Claude Code accounts as follows:  

1. **Consumer Accounts (Free/Pro/Max):**  
   - Governed by the [Consumer Terms of Service](https://www.anthropic.com/legal/consumer-terms).  
   - Focus on basic usage rules, with fewer restrictions on data handling.  

2. **Commercial Accounts (Team/Enterprise/API):**  
   - Subject to the [Commercial Terms](https://www.anthropic.com/legal/commercial-terms).  
   - Include stricter data usage policies, such as:  
     - Explicit data ownership and usage restrictions.  
     - Compliance with industry regulations (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA).  
     - Additional obligations for API integrations and third-party access.  

For full details, refer to the [Legal and Compliance](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/legal-and-compliance) section in the Claude Code documentation.

records.answer:
To create a custom hook for auto-formatting code after Claude edits:
1. Define a `command` hook in `/hooks` directory with `event: "edit"`
2. Specify your formatter (e.g., Prettier/Black) in the `command` field
3. Use matcher patterns to target code files (e.g., `*.py`, `*.js`)
4. Ensure exit code handling for formatting errors
Documentation: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks
